summary:
The Burning of Books and Burying of Scholars (213–210 BC) was [[event:3141|Qin Shi Huang]]'s systematic suppression of intellectual diversity, in which the chancellor Li Si ordered the destruction of books outside the [[event:1113|Legalist]] canon and the execution of scholars who continued to discuss outlawed texts — one of history's most notorious acts of cultural repression.

The campaign began when court scholars debated whether the Qin should follow the ancient Zhou practice of enfeoffing relatives as regional lords. Li Si argued this was dangerous precedent and proposed burning all books except practical manuals on agriculture, medicine, and divination, as well as official Qin historical records. The Confucian classics, philosophical texts, and historical records of the other states were to be surrendered for burning within thirty days. Scholars who continued to discuss the proscribed texts were to be branded and sent to forced labor; those who 'dared to use the past to criticize the present' were to be executed along with their families. Ancient sources claim 460 scholars were subsequently buried alive (or executed) for defying the edict. The campaign's ultimate irony: it failed to destroy the texts it targeted, as copies were hidden, and the rapid collapse of the Qin dynasty after the emperor's death allowed the suppressed traditions — especially Confucianism — to re-emerge under the [[event:77|Han Dynasty]].
